repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
<!--
  Thank you for contributing to Neovim!
  If this is your first time, check out https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#pull-requests-prs
  for our PR guidelines.
-->

## Problem
On Windows 10, the ConPTY closing order is strict. Calling ClosePseudoConsole after closing the output stream can easily lead to accidental deadlocks, leaving orphan conhost.exe processes.
See https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/console/closepseudoconsole#remarks
and https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/console/creating-a-pseudoconsole-session#ending-the-pseudoconsole-session

## Solution
Based on the warning in the docs above, we need to call ClosePseudoConsole on a separate thread first so that the output pipe can fully drain on the main thread.
Also, `wait_eof_timer` is outdated, so I removed it to avoid extra cleanup logic around it (it was introduced in the early winpty days).


fix #36395, fix #21830, fix #12974

comment_data.body: This condition is strange. Why does the behavior of closing a console depend on whether it has written output?
Ah, I'm trying to catch where the terminal opens and closes immediately, but I haven't found a better way. Any ideas? :D
Ah, I don't have any other ideas for now.

should this be done in `stream_may_close` (or `stream_close_handle` ) ? otherwise it may be called even if the stream was already closed. Or is that intentional?

also might as well pass `stream->s` instead of only passing `stream->s.internal_data` , since that's more consistent?

should this be on `stream` rather than `rstream`? even if it's not needed for other kinds of streams yet, it's confusing to have all these special properties in random places.

also seems like the convention is to suffix with `_cb` so i guess let's keep that .
One question is why can't the existing `close_cb` do this, i.e. why do we need `before_close_cb`? Probably I just need to trace the code to understand it.

comment_regular_data.body: This PR still fixes #36395 on **Windows 10**, but I can't guarantee all existing terminal-related tests will pass there since quite a few were already failing anyway; to make things more reliable, I've set up a Windows Server 2025 VM so I can run the functional tests locally before pushing changes (same as CI OS version) 😂
For now, I haven't come up with a good way to test for ohphan processes. If anyone has good ideas, I'd be happy to try adding a new test. :D

In the Solution description can you mention that the timer was dropped (good!), and why a thread is preferred:

> Call ClosePseudoConsole on a separate thread firstly, so the main thread can flush the final frame. *Drop the old timer approach, which unlike a timer [has problem X...]*

@justinmk, haha, your comments are spot on! I was digging into this issue and encountered so many concepts that my mind was doing somersaults—I didn't even manage to put the changes in the best spot. :D

This is risky, but worth it because Windows support needs the attention. And the simplified code is nice.

Thank you!

> This is risky, but worth it because Windows support needs the attention. And the simplified code is nice.
> 
> Thank you!

All my PRs come with 1-year warranty and 24/7 support — including any new regressions 😉

title: fix(pty): prevent orphan conhost.exe on Windows 10
